agent_output: {"handoff_brief":{"recipient":"Shaq","task_context":"Implement API rate limiting middleware. Four agents have analyzed this feature: Bird defined the domain rules (100 req/60s rolling window per API key, 429 + Retry-After on exceed), MJ proposed Redis-based sliding window middleware, Pippen added operational requirements (fail-open, metrics), and Kobe reviewed the design and caught a critical flaw in the window algorithm. Shaq should implement the middleware using Kobe's corrected approach (Redis sorted sets) rather than MJ's original fixed-window key pattern.","domain_rules":[{"rule":"Rate limit is 100 requests per 60-second rolling window per API key","testable_assertion":"Given API key K, if 100 requests arrive within any 60-second sliding window, request 101 returns HTTP 429"},{"rule":"Exceeding the limit returns HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header","testable_assertion":"The 429 response includes a Retry-After header whose value is the number of seconds until enough old requests expire to allow a new one"},{"rule":"The window is rolling, not fixed","testable_assertion":"Two bursts of 60 requests each, 30 seconds apart, must trigger rate limiting on the second burst (combined 120 in a 60s window) — a fixed-window implementation would incorrectly allow both bursts"},{"rule":"Rate limiter fails open when Redis is unavailable","testable_assertion":"If Redis is down, requests are allowed through (not rejected) and a redis_unavailable_total metric is incremented"}],"architecture_guidance":[{"decision":"Use Redis sorted sets for true sliding window rate limiting","implementation_note":"MJ's original design used a key pattern rate_limit:{api_key}:{window_start_unix} with TTL — Kobe identified this as a fixed window susceptible to boundary bursts. Instead: use a sorted set per API key (key: rate_limit:{api_key}), add each request as a member with score = current unix timestamp in ms, ZREMRANGEBYSCORE to prune entries older than 60s, ZCARD to count. Wrap in a MULTI/EXEC or Lua script for atomicity. Set a 60s TTL on the sorted set key as a safety net."},{"decision":"Middleware placement: before all authenticated routes","implementation_note":"The rate limit check runs after authentication (so the API key is available) but before any route handler logic. Extract API key from the auth context, not from raw headers."},{"decision":"Fail-open on Redis failure","implementation_note":"Wrap all Redis calls in try/catch. On any Redis error, log it, increment redis_unavailable_total counter, and allow the request through. Do NOT reject requests when Redis is unreachable."},{"decision":"Retry-After must be dynamically computed","implementation_note":"Compute Retry-After as: the timestamp of the oldest request still in the sorted set window minus (now - 60s), effectively the number of seconds until the earliest entry expires and frees a slot. Do NOT use a fixed value like 60."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"criterion":"101st request in 60s window is rejected","given":"An API key has made 100 requests in the last 60 seconds","when":"A 101st request arrives","then":"HTTP 429 is returned with a Retry-After header showing seconds until a slot frees up"},{"criterion":"Window resets after expiry","given":"An API key hit the limit 60 seconds ago and made no further requests","when":"A new request arrives","then":"The request succeeds with HTTP 200 (or appropriate success code)"},{"criterion":"Rolling window prevents boundary burst","given":"An API key sends 90 requests at second 55 of window W","when":"10 more requests arrive at second 5 of the next fixed window (still within 60s of the first batch)","then":"The combined count exceeds 100 and requests beyond the limit return 429"},{"criterion":"Fail-open when Redis is down","given":"Redis is unavailable","when":"Any request arrives","then":"The request is allowed through, and redis_unavailable_total metric is incremented"},{"criterion":"Metrics are emitted","given":"Normal operation","when":"A request is rate-limited","then":"rate_limit_exceeded_total counter is incremented with the API key as a label"}],"terminology_alignment":[{"domain_term":"rolling window","technical_term":"sliding window via Redis sorted set","definition":"A 60-second window that slides continuously with time, not aligned to fixed boundaries. Each request is evaluated against the count of requests in the preceding 60 seconds from the current moment."},{"domain_term":"API key","technical_term":"authenticated principal identifier","definition":"The key extracted from the auth context that uniquely identifies the caller. Rate limits are scoped per API key."},{"domain_term":"Retry-After","technical_term":"TTL of oldest sorted set entry relative to window boundary","definition":"The number of whole seconds the client should wait before retrying. Computed dynamically from the oldest request in the current window, not a fixed constant."}],"contradictions_resolved":[{"conflict":"MJ proposed a 'sliding window counter' using key pattern rate_limit:{api_key}:{window_start_unix} with TTL. Kobe identified this as a fixed-window algorithm, not a true sliding window, which allows burst-at-boundary attacks (up to 2x the limit across a boundary).","resolution":"Adopted Kobe's recommendation: use Redis sorted sets (ZADD + ZREMRANGEBYSCORE + ZCARD) for a true sliding window that satisfies Bird's 'rolling window' domain rule. MJ's key-per-window approach is discarded."},{"conflict":"Pippen stated Retry-After should be 'TTL of the current window key' (which assumed MJ's fixed-window key pattern). With sorted sets, there is no single window key with a TTL.","resolution":"Retry-After is computed as the time until the oldest entry in the sorted set ages out of the 60s window, freeing a slot. This is consistent with Pippen's intent (dynamic, not fixed) but adapted to the sorted set implementation."}],"open_questions":[]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":90,"high_confidence_areas":["Domain rules are clear and unambiguous from Bird","Sorted set approach is well-established for sliding window rate limiting","Fail-open and metrics requirements are concrete and testable","All four agents are aligned on the what — disagreements were only on the how, and are resolved"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the existing codebase already has Redis as a dependency or if setup/connection config is needed","Whether rate limiting should also apply to unauthenticated routes (Bird scoped to API key only)","Whether the Lua script vs MULTI/EXEC choice has performance implications at expected load"],"assumptions":["Redis is available as infrastructure (or will be provisioned as part of this work)","API keys are already extracted by an auth middleware upstream","The 100 req/60s limit is uniform across all API keys — no per-tier differentiation yet"]}}

Four agents have analyzed this feature: Bird defined the domain rules (100 req/60s rolling window per API key, 429 + Retry-After on exceed), MJ proposed Redis-based sliding window middleware, Pippen added operational requirements (fail-open, metrics), and Kobe reviewed the design and caught a critical flaw in the window algorithm. Shaq should implement the middleware using Kobe's corrected approach (Redi

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the four agent outputs for contradictions, missing inputs, and terminology mismatches before producing the handoff brief.

Key contradiction detected: Kobe flags that MJ's sliding window counter with `window_start_unix` keys is actually a fixed window counter (susceptible to boundary bursts), not a true sliding window. Kobe recommends sorted sets instead.

Wait - is this a contradiction or a refinement? MJ says "sliding window counter" but the key pattern `rate_limit:{api_key}:{window_start_unix}` is actually a fixed window approach. 

Kobe's pointing out a design flaw here—MJ and Kobe both want Redis and a middleware approach, but Kobe's catching that MJ's implementation doesn't actually deliver the sliding window behavior it claims. This is a technical refinement rather than a fundamental disagreement about direction. The stop condition requires escalations with type "contradiction" to block the handoff brief, but this feels more like Kobe improving MJ's design than a true contradiction that needs escalation.

Since Bird's domain rule explicitly calls for a "rolling window," Kobe's sorted set approach is the right implementation—it supersedes MJ's fixed window approach. I'll treat this as resolved and move forward with the handoff brief.
